nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats, seagrass beds, and rubble zones; tends to prefer somewhat shaded or turbid conditions in its native range
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; creeping stolon anchors to sand, rubble, or rock via rhizoids; forms dense mats under suitable conditions; native populations kept in check by herbivorous fish absent from introduced regions

avoidWith: tanks with no light schedule control

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Coughdrop12; 2048x1536. Vibrant, sharp compact green mound of Caulerpa brachypus in a reef aquarium; clean single subject showing the diagnostic small tongue/strap-shaped fronds. Best display shot.

Photo by Coughdrop12; 4032x3024. Aquarium shot showing individual short tongue-shaped fronds spreading from a creeping stolon over sand/rock (diagnostic morphology); slight water haze so kept as non-recommended.

Photo by Shaun Lee; 2000x1333. In-situ natural reef growth showing the short tongue-shaped fronds arranged along a stolon; adds native-habitat context but subject is somewhat turf-covered and dull, so non-recommended.

summary: Mini Caulerpa (Caulerpa brachypus) is a small green macroalga from the Indo-Pacific, with short tongue-shaped fronds up to 5 cm tall. Fast-growing and well suited to reef systems and refugiums, but a confirmed invasive in Florida and the Caribbean — captive specimens must never be released.
careNotes:
Mini Caulerpa (Caulerpa brachypus) is a green macroalga distinguished by its unusually small stature — short, blade-like (tongue or strap-shaped) fronds typically reaching 3–5 cm, making it noticeably compact compared to close relatives such as C. prolifera. Like all Caulerpa, it is a siphonous alga, meaning the entire plant body is technically a single cell containing many nuclei rather than being divided into separate cells as most plants are. It spreads by a creeping stolon (a horizontal runner) that anchors to sand, rubble, or rockwork via fine root-like rhizoids. Fronds are generally green, sometimes with yellowish margins.

Mini Caulerpa generally does best under medium-to-high reef lighting with at least 8 hours of photoperiod, or continuous 24-hour lighting for refugium use (see the GOING SEXUAL section below). Moderate water flow and stable reef water parameters are recommended. The species has been reported to tolerate a range of conditions but generally performs best in the 22–26°C range typical of tropical reef systems.

ALLELOPATHY: Like other Caulerpa species, C. brachypus is likely to release secondary metabolites including caulerpin and related compounds. These have been documented to stress or inhibit nearby corals and competing algae. Placement near sensitive SPS corals is generally not advised; running activated carbon is commonly recommended by keepers of allelopathic macroalgae.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: Under stress — particularly sharp nutrient drops, photoperiod disruption, or rapid temperature changes — Caulerpa species can undergo holocarpy, in which the entire thallus converts to gametes and effectively disintegrates. This can release large quantities of organic matter, causing acute water-quality crashes (ammonia spikes, oxygen depletion) capable of devastating a reef tank. Mitigations: run refugium specimens on 24-hour lighting; harvest regularly; maintain stable moderate nutrient levels; remove immediately any specimen showing white/translucent discoloration or turbid release.

INVASIVE STATUS — CRITICAL: Caulerpa brachypus is a confirmed invasive species in Florida (first detected in Martin County in 1999; spread through the Indian River Lagoon by 2003), Martinique, and New Zealand. In Florida it has been documented causing severe reef degradation — eliminating fish and lobster populations in affected areas — earning the descriptor 'killer algae.' In its native Indo-Pacific range, populations are regulated by herbivorous fish; those natural predators are absent in introduced regions, allowing unchecked growth. NEVER release C. brachypus or any fragment into natural waterways, sewage, or storm drains. Caulerpa species are BANNED for sale in California; check local regulations before purchase.

UPON ARRIVAL: Fronds commonly appear white or translucent after shipping due to chloroplast displacement in darkness — this is normal and generally reverses within hours of light exposure.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily via fragmentation and vegetative tip growth along the stolon — even small fragments have been reported capable of establishing new colonies. The species' invasive success in Florida and other introduced regions is largely attributable to its vigorous vegetative propagation.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source for this species. Product page confirms blade form, prolific growth, display suitability, and invasive designation. Minimal husbandry parameter data; general Caulerpa care extrapolated from genus-level sources.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa brachypus
additional.notes: Taxonomic cross-check (Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales confirmed). Native range (Indo-Pacific: East Africa, India, SE Asia, Pacific Islands, W. Australia) confirmed. Invasion history in Florida (1999), Martinique, New Zealand documented. Morphology: tongue/strap-like fronds up to 5 cm, creeping stolon. Depth range: 25–47 m native but thrives in shallower turbid waters in introduced range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa (genus)
additional.notes: Cross-check on genus-level care: allelopathic compounds (caulerpin, caulerchlorin, caulerpicin), rapid growth via fragmentation, stolon/rhizoid anchoring morphology, and holocarpy reproductive biology corroborated.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
